State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, CA 95814 • 1107 9TH STREET, SUITE 320 SACRAMENTO

July 31, 1979

Mayor Russell Boarisch
Box 1786
Palm Springs, CA 92263

Dear Mr. Boarisch:

Thank you for your letter of June 27, 1979. In that letter
you expressed concern with regard to the application of the
Political Reform Act to "Advisory Groups" in
your city which have no decision-making authority.

The Political Reform Act requires every state and local agency
to adopt a conflict of interest code. Financial disclosure
is required of those employees who have the authority to make
governmental decisions which may result in financial interests.
Employees who do not possess decision-making
authority, are not required to disclose their financial in-
terests under the agency's conflict of interest code.

In your letter, you make reference to members of your city's
Youth Commission who may be required to disclose their financial
interests and those of their spouses. Initially we observe that members of the Commission should be required to
disclose their financial interests only if, under your Commission's code adopted by the Council, it is determined
that they have the ability to make decisions which could
affect financial interests. If such a determination is made,
then the Act requires full financial disclosure. If the
ordinance that described in the statute, none of the appointees
you may name to serve on boards in such financial manner, financial interests.

As mentioned previously, if you have questions regarding
a conflict of interest code, the Finance Committee has interpreted
this requirement to mean that an agency's decision-making
authority must make financial disclosures. The Advisory

Mr. Russell Ostrich
July 31, 1979                                 [illegible] Pres

the Commission has asked an adoption rule whereby
agencies which will be directed in six months of the
not possess deviation-making information upon a whole
conflict of interest ones. For oration [illegible] Admin.
Code Sec. 1873 which I have modified, the oral Court of
of Palm Springs may wish to apply a further rule.

I hope that the advice I am herewith is in the letter
has been helpful. If we can be of further assistance
regarding this matter please do not hesitate to contact our
office.

[illegible]

[illegible] F. [illegible]
[illegible] General
Counsel and [illegible]

Enclosure
Memo

CITY OF PALM SPRINGS
CALIFORNIA
Box 1786, 92264

Office of the
Mayor (714) 325 8411

June 27, 1978

Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman, Fair Political
Practices Commission
1100 K Street Building
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Chairman Lowenstein:

The City is presently going through the process of having
our Advisory Boards and Commissions, number eleven, adopt
Conflict of Interest Codes as provided by State law and
have run into a situation wherein the members are reacting
adversely. I must agree that it does seem that the point
has been stretched when we are asking appointed members of
Advisory Boards, with no decision making ability, and who
are donating their time to the community, to endure the paper
work process. To illustrate, it has been indicated that for
members of our Youth Commission, who are under eighteen and
dependent upon their parents for support, the Conflict of
Interest Codes would cover their parents as far as reporting
and abstaining and I find this absolutely ludicrous because
I can't believe that was ever intended.

I feel very strongly that provisions of Proposition 9, as
applied to elected officials, and professional personnel working for local government makes some sense but to stretch it
to the Advisory Groups, who statewide are probably not that
effective really doesn't make that much sense. As you can
tell, I am frustrated and my frustration is exceeded only by
that of the members of our Advisory Boards who feel they are
being asked to do something that has no relationship to their
performance.

CITY OF PALM SPRINGS


page two

Mr. Daniel Lowenstein


I would certainly appreciate whatever information, and
please don't send it to me in bureaucratic language, that
might give us some guidance in this particular area and
if you have any suggestions based on what other communities
have done to alleviate or solve the problem, it would be
greatly appreciated.

Very truly yours,


RUSSELL J. BETRICH
Mayor


RJB/mm
CC: City Council

embedding.qa_text: **State of California** **Fair Political Practices Commission** P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95814 • 1011 L Street Building, Sacramento, CA July 31, 1978 Mayor Russell Beirich Box 1786 Palm Springs, CA 92262 Dear Mr. Beirich: Thank you for your letter of June 27, 1978. In that letter you expressed concern with respect to the application of the Political Reform Act (Proposition 9) to "Advisory Groups" in your city which have no decision-making authority. The Political Reform Act requires every state and local agency to adopt a conflict of interest code. Financial disclosure is required of those employees who have the authority to make governmental decisions which may materially affect financial interests. Employees who do not possess decision-making authority, are not required to disclose their financial interests under the agency's conflict of interest code. In your letter, you make a reference to members of your city's Youth Commission who may be required to disclose their financial interests and those of their parents. Initially we observe that members of the Commission should be required to disclose their financial interests only after the Youth Commission's code reviewing body (City Council) has determined that they have the ability to make decisions which could affect financial interests. If the code reviewing body concludes that disclosure by the commission members and other employees is necessary, the Act does not require that the dependent child who may be dependent on the code disclose his or her parent's financial interests. As mentioned previously, the Commission has interpreted this requirement to mean that an agency with decision-making authority must adopt a conflict of interest policy. Accordingly Mr. Russell Bourich July 31, 1978 the Commission has adopted an adoption policy whereby agencies which will be dissolved in six months or do not possess decision-making authority need not adopt a conflict of interest code. See regulation 611. Admin. Code Sec. 18751 which I have enclosed. The City Council of Palm Springs may wish to adopt a similar policy. I hope that the advice that has been offered in this letter has been helpful. If we may be of further assistance regarding this matter please do not hesitate to contact our office. Sincerely, Robert E. Haggerty General Counsel Conflict of Interest Division Enclosure DED:nw City of Palm Springs CALIFORNIA Office of the Mayor (714) 323-8201 June 27, 1978 Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein Chairman, Fair Political Practices Commission 1100 K Street Building Sacramento, California 95814 Dear Chairman Lowenstein: The City is presently going through the process of having our Advisory Boards and Commissions, numbering eleven, adopt Conflict of Interest Codes as provided by State law and have run into a situation wherein the members are reacting adversely. I must agree that it does seem that the point has been stretched when we are asking appointed members of Advisory Boards, with no decision making ability, and who are donating their time to the community, to endure the paper work process. To illustrate, it has been indicated that for members of our Youth Commission,
